title: Great British Inventions and Notable People
overview: Britain has made extraordinary contributions to science, technology and culture. This lesson covers the great British inventions of the 20th century, notable figures from recent British history, and a few important people from the industrial era who were missed in other lessons. The handbook explicitly states you must know about 20th century inventions — this is highly testable material.

study_sections.heading: Great British Inventions of the 20th Century
study_sections.content:
Britain has given the world some wonderful inventions. Key examples from the 20th century include:

Television: developed by Scotsman John Logie Baird (1888-1946) in the 1920s. In 1932 he made the first television broadcast between London and Glasgow.

Radar: developed by Scotsman Sir Robert Watson-Watt (1892-1973), who proposed that enemy aircraft could be detected by radio waves. The first successful radar test took place in 1935.

Jodrell Bank Radio Telescope: Sir Bernard Lovell (1913-2012) built a radio telescope at Jodrell Bank in Cheshire. For many years it was the biggest in the world and continues to operate today.

The Turing Machine: a theoretical mathematical device invented by Alan Turing (1912-54) in the 1930s, influential in the development of computer science and the modern-day computer.

Insulin: Scottish physician John MacLeod (1876-1935) was the co-discoverer of insulin, used to treat diabetes.

DNA structure: discovered in 1953 through work at British universities in London and Cambridge. Francis Crick (1916-2004), one of those awarded the Nobel Prize, was British.

Jet engine: developed in Britain in the 1930s by Sir Frank Whittle (1907-96), a Royal Air Force engineer officer.

Hovercraft: invented in the 1950s by Sir Christopher Cockerell (1910-99).

Concorde: Britain and France jointly developed the world's only supersonic passenger aircraft. It first flew in 1969, began carrying passengers in 1976, and was retired from service in 2003.

Harrier jump jet: an aircraft capable of taking off vertically, designed and developed in the UK.

Cashpoint/ATM: in the 1960s, James Goodfellow (1937-) invented the cash-dispensing ATM. The first was put into use by Barclays Bank in Enfield, north London in 1967.

IVF therapy: pioneered in Britain by Sir Robert Edwards and Patrick Steptoe. The world's first 'test-tube baby' was born in Oldham, Lancashire in 1978.

Dolly the sheep: in 1996, Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell led the team that first succeeded in cloning a mammal.

MRI scanner: Sir Peter Mansfield is the co-inventor of the MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) scanner.

World Wide Web: invented by Sir Tim Berners-Lee (1955-). Information was successfully transferred via the web for the first time on 25 December 1990.

study_sections.heading: Notable People: Modern Era
study_sections.content:
Margaret Thatcher (1925-2013) was the daughter of a grocer from Grantham in Lincolnshire. She became the first woman Prime Minister of the UK in 1979 and was the longest-serving Prime Minister of the 20th century, remaining in office until 1990. She worked closely with US President Ronald Reagan.

Roald Dahl (1916-90) was born in Wales to Norwegian parents. He served in the Royal Air Force in World War Two before becoming a celebrated author. His best-known works include Charlie and the Chocolate Factory and George's Marvellous Medicine. Several of his books have been made into films.

Mary Peters (1939-) was born in Manchester but moved to Northern Ireland as a child. She won an Olympic gold medal in the pentathlon in 1972. She went on to raise money for local athletics and became team manager for the women's British Olympic team. She continues to promote sport and tourism in Northern Ireland and was made a Dame of the British Empire in 2000.

study_sections.heading: Sake Dean Mahomet - Industrial Era
study_sections.content: Sake Dean Mahomet (1759-1851) was born in Bengal, India. He served in the Bengal army and came to Britain in 1782. In 1810, he opened the Hindoostane Coffee House in George Street, London — the first curry house to open in Britain. Mahomet and his wife also introduced 'shampooing' (the Indian art of head massage) to Britain.

key_facts: John Logie Baird developed television in the 1920s and made the first broadcast between London and Glasgow in 1932.
Sir Robert Watson-Watt developed radar. The first successful radar test took place in 1935.
Sir Bernard Lovell built the Jodrell Bank radio telescope in Cheshire - for many years the biggest in the world.
Alan Turing invented the theoretical Turing Machine in the 1930s, influential in computer science.
John MacLeod was the co-discoverer of insulin.
Francis Crick was British and won the Nobel Prize for discovering the structure of DNA in 1953.
Sir Frank Whittle developed the jet engine in the 1930s.
Sir Christopher Cockerell invented the hovercraft in the 1950s.
Concorde first flew in 1969, began carrying passengers in 1976, and was retired in 2003.
James Goodfellow invented the ATM. The first cashpoint was used by Barclays Bank in Enfield in 1967.
The world's first IVF baby was born in Oldham, Lancashire in 1978.
In 1996, Dolly the sheep was the first mammal to be cloned.
Sir Peter Mansfield is the co-inventor of the MRI scanner.
Sir Tim Berners-Lee invented the World Wide Web. Information was first transferred on 25 December 1990.
Margaret Thatcher was the first woman Prime Minister and the longest-serving PM of the 20th century.
Roald Dahl was born in Wales and wrote Charlie and the Chocolate Factory.
Mary Peters won Olympic gold in the pentathlon in 1972 and was made a Dame in 2000.
Sake Dean Mahomet opened the first curry house in Britain in 1810 and introduced shampooing.
memory_hook: Inventions chronologically: 'TV (Baird, 1920s), Radar (Watson-Watt, 1935), Jet (Whittle, 1930s), Hovercraft (Cockerell, 1950s), DNA (Crick, 1953), Concorde (1969/1976), ATM (1967), IVF (1978), Dolly (1996), Web (Berners-Lee, 1990)'. Remember: first curry house = Mahomet 1810.

questions.prompt: Who developed television?
questions.explanation: The television was developed by Scotsman John Logie Baird in the 1920s. In 1932 he made the first broadcast between London and Glasgow.

questions.prompt: Who developed radar?
questions.explanation: Radar was developed by Scotsman Sir Robert Watson-Watt. The first successful radar test took place in 1935.

questions.prompt: What is at Jodrell Bank in Cheshire?
options: A radio telescope, for many years the biggest in the world
questions.explanation: Sir Bernard Lovell built a radio telescope at Jodrell Bank in Cheshire which was for many years the biggest in the world.

questions.prompt: Who invented the Turing Machine?
questions.explanation: Alan Turing (1912-54) invented the theoretical Turing Machine in the 1930s, which was influential in the development of computer science.

questions.prompt: Who developed the jet engine?
questions.explanation: The jet engine was developed in Britain in the 1930s by Sir Frank Whittle, a Royal Air Force engineer officer.

questions.prompt: Who invented the hovercraft?
questions.explanation: Sir Christopher Cockerell (1910-99) invented the hovercraft in the 1950s.

questions.prompt: When did Concorde first fly?
questions.explanation: Concorde first flew in 1969. It began carrying passengers in 1976 and was retired from service in 2003.

questions.prompt: When did Concorde begin carrying passengers?
questions.explanation: Concorde first flew in 1969 but began carrying passengers in 1976.

questions.prompt: Who invented the ATM (cashpoint)?
questions.explanation: James Goodfellow (1937-) invented the ATM in the 1960s. The first cashpoint was used by Barclays Bank in Enfield, north London in 1967.

questions.prompt: Where was the world's first IVF baby born?
questions.explanation: The world's first 'test-tube baby' was born in Oldham, Lancashire in 1978.

questions.prompt: What was Dolly the sheep?
options: The first mammal to be cloned, in 1996
questions.explanation: In 1996, Sir Ian Wilmot and Keith Campbell led the team that first cloned a mammal - Dolly the sheep.

questions.prompt: What did Sir Peter Mansfield co-invent?
questions.explanation: Sir Peter Mansfield is the co-inventor of the MRI (magnetic resonance imaging) scanner.

questions.prompt: Who invented the World Wide Web?
questions.explanation: The inventor of the World Wide Web is Sir Tim Berners-Lee, who is British.

questions.prompt: When was information first successfully transferred via the web?
questions.explanation: Information was successfully transferred via the web for the first time on 25 December 1990.

questions.prompt: Who was the first woman Prime Minister of the UK?
questions.explanation: Margaret Thatcher became the first woman Prime Minister of the UK in 1979.

questions.prompt: Who was the longest-serving PM of the 20th century?
questions.explanation: Margaret Thatcher was the longest-serving Prime Minister of the 20th century, remaining in office from 1979 to 1990.

questions.prompt: Where was Roald Dahl born?
questions.explanation: Roald Dahl (1916-90) was born in Wales to Norwegian parents.

questions.prompt: What sport did Mary Peters win Olympic gold in?
questions.explanation: Mary Peters won an Olympic gold medal in the pentathlon in 1972.

questions.prompt: What was Sake Dean Mahomet famous for?
options: Opening the first curry house in Britain (1810) and introducing shampooing
Being the first Indian to be elected to Parliament
Founding the first Indian restaurant chain
Writing the first British book about Indian culture
questions.explanation: Sake Dean Mahomet opened the Hindoostane Coffee House in London in 1810 - the first curry house in Britain. He and his wife also introduced shampooing (Indian head massage) to Britain.

questions.prompt: Who was the co-discoverer of insulin?
questions.explanation: Scottish physician John MacLeod (1876-1935) was the co-discoverer of insulin, used to treat diabetes.

questions.prompt: Who won the Nobel Prize for discovering the structure of DNA?
questions.explanation: The structure of the DNA molecule was discovered in 1953. Francis Crick (1916-2004), one of those awarded the Nobel Prize, was British.

flashcards: Built by Sir Bernard Lovell, Cheshire - was world's biggest
Alan Turing (1930s) - led to computer science
James Goodfellow (1960s, first at Barclays Enfield 1967)
Who invented the World Wide Web?
First curry house in Britain (1810), introduced shampooing
